 n = 49 (number of births)  
 p = 0.50 (probability a birth is a boy)  
 q = 1 – p = 0.50  

a) Mean  
 μ = n p = 49(0.50) = 24.5 boys  

b) Standard deviation  
 σ = √(n p q) = √[49(0.50)(0.50)] = √(12.25) = 3.5 boys  

c) Can we use a normal approximation?  
 Check n p = 24.5 ≥ 10 and n q = 24.5 ≥ 10.  
 Both conditions hold, so the binomial may be approximated by N(μ = 24.5, σ = 3.5).

d) Probability at least 23 boys (X ≥ 23) using the normal approximation  
 Apply a continuity correction: P(X ≥ 23) ≈ P(X ≥ 22.5).

 z = (22.5 – 24.5)/3.5 = –2 / 3.5 ≈ –0.5714

 P(X ≥ 22.5) = P(Z ≥ –0.5714)  

 P(Z ≥ –0.5714) = 1 – Φ(–0.5714) = 1 – (1 – Φ(0.5714)) = Φ(0.5714)

 From the standard normal table, Φ(0.5714) ≈ 0.716.

Therefore, using the normal approximation, the probability that at least 23 of the 49 births are boys is approximately 0.72 (about 72%).
